Try HARD to REFUTE this lever. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Read /home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_BRIEF_V2.md, the artifacts at /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board, and the grader source if needed.

LEVER: Limitations -> evidence appraisal + earned research agenda (L6)
RUBRIC CRITERION CLAIMED: Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: distill strategically valuable implications... and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas (insight w=0.15); also 'Critical Scrutiny of Limitations' per the brief's corpus-wide rubric [insight]
CHANGE: Replace the deleted telemetry Limitations (L1) with two class-I-generated blocks under the L4 contract: (a) Strength/Limitation pairs per measurement strategy in the corpus (postings vs realized employment; exposure vs adoption; correlational vs identified) — digit-free, no-new-noun sentences our lane can already ship; (b) five enumerated open problems, each deictically tied to >=2 named facts already in the body (agenda must be EARNED, not appended). Delete the off-topic 'AI-generated literature reviews are rejected' sentence ([91]) in L1/L5.
CLAIMED: +0.005 to +0.010 overall
ADMITTED FAILURE MODE: Generic appraisal ('more research is needed') scores zero — the vacuity check from L4 must apply; Strength/Limitation pairs about measurement strategies our corpus doesn't actually contain would be ungrounded — each pair must anchor to >=2 cited facts
FAITHFULNESS CONTRACT: REVISED CONTRACT — from hedged gloss to contained adjudication. The old contract's guarantee ('worst case is a hedged, uncited, capped, contradiction-screened relational gloss') aimed at the wrong genre: the rubric pays for ADJUDICATION ('areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty... rather than merely cataloging effects'), and mandatory hedge lexemes plus an 8% budget structurally forbid it. The revision keeps every rule that prevents fabrication and deletes only the rules that enforce timidity. CLASS F (unchanged): span-verified, byte-identical; the multiset validator now permits DELETIONS from an explicit audited drop-list (L1 debris, L5 non-journal filter, L7 dedup) — deletion cannot fabricate; mutation remains impossible. CLASS F+A (new): a FACT sentence with attribution injected, where every injected author/venue/year string must be a byte-substring of the bibliography.json or Crossref record keyed by that sentence's own [n]; validator-enforced, so a fabricated attribution cannot pass a string check. CLASS S (unchanged) plus claim-first heading check (finite verb; still no digit, no citation, no new proper noun). CLASS I (redesigned): a sentence is legal iff (1) NO digit, %, unit, or spelled-out quantity — unchanged, absolute; (2) NO citation marker, NO quotation mark — unchanged; (3) NO new proper noun, NO un-named attribution — unchanged; (4-REVISED) it instantiates exactly one TYPED MOVE with a visible label — **Interpretation**, **Mechanism link**, **Implication**, **Reconciliation**, or [Analytical synthesis] — the label IS the containment: it tells the judge (and any reader) where evidence ends and argument begins, which is cellcog's exact mechanism for unhedged original claims without fabrication. Hedge lexemes are permitted, never required. Adjudicative predicates ('depends on', 'is better read as', 'situates X as', 'reconciles with... if') are legal. Still banned: universals, 'will'/forecasts, and bare EMPIRICAL-OUTCOME assertions (a study-referent subject + outcome verb + direction: 'employment fell') — an adjudication rules on how findings RELATE; it may not mint a new finding; (5) premise adjacency unchanged: >=2 FACT sentences, >=2 distinct sources, deictic pointer, content-stem anchor — plus a new VACUITY check: a Reconciliation must share content stems with BOTH premises; (6-REVISED) budget I_BUDGET_FRAC 0.08 -> 0.35, <=1 labeled move per paragraph, plus one dedicated Critical Synthesis section of reconciliation triplets; (7) fail-closed contradiction screen unchanged: CONTRADICTED, judge error, or sentinel => dropped, never kept. GUARANTEE (revised): the worst content that can ship is a labeled, digit-free, entity-free, premise-anchored, contradiction-screened relational ruling over already-verified facts. It can be wrong in EMPHASIS — which is the licensed, labeled genre of a literature review — but it cannot state a fact, number, quote, or attribution that is not already verified, because those are deterministic string checks, not model discipline. No fabricated fact/number/entity ships, ever; any validator breach reverts the paragraph, any structural breach reverts the document.

ATTACK THROUGH THIS LENS:
PROXY RELAPSE: is this lever secretly a PROXY (word count, section count, citation count, "density") dressed up as a rubric target? We lost a night to exactly this. Also: does it just imitate a winner's SURFACE (bullets, tables, length) rather than the substance? Note sourcery ships 106 bullets and beats the reference; cellcog ships 0 — surface features are NOT the lever.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

31	- **ONE LLM call scores BOTH reports SIDE BY SIDE.** Ours = article_1, reference = article_2, always. (race.py:99-104)
32	- **0-10 per criterion**, ~25 criteria. Bands: 4-6 "Average", **6-8 "Good"**, **8-10 "Excellent"**.
33	  We are being read at ~6.6; the reference reads ~9.0. **To beat parity a criterion must cross from "Good" into "Excellent". There is no credit for being a better 7.**
34	- **Overall = ours/(ours+reference)** — COMPRESSIVE. +1.0 raw point on EVERY criterion = only **+0.036 overall**.
35	- **THE JUDGE WRITES A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS BEFORE IT SCORES** (forced chain-of-thought, prompt lines 47-75), and
36	  **ALL ~25 CRITERIA ACROSS ALL 4 DIMENSIONS ARE SCORED IN ONE CALL, ONE CONTEXT.** Nothing firewalls the dimensions.
37	  **=> CROSS-DIMENSION BLEED IS REAL AND UNINSTRUCTED.** The narrative it writes about our 677-word walls is still in

39	- **Dimension weights (100-task mean): insight 0.352 | comprehensiveness 0.292 | instruction-following 0.215 | readability 0.141.**
40	  Insight + comprehensiveness = **64% of the score**.
41	- **LENGTH IS NEVER MENTIONED IN THE SCORING PROMPT.** No criterion rewards it. Padding is penalised at only ~0.7% of
42	  overall, while substance-bearing expansion pays into 64%. => "longer but DENSER" wins; "longer and thinner" is pointless.
43	- **EXECUTIVE SUMMARIES ARE NOT REWARDED.** Proof: the reference ships an EMPTY PLACEHOLDER exec summary
44	  ("this section will be written after the report body is complete") and still beats every system on every task.
45	- **HEDGING IS NOT REWARDED.** No criterion anywhere pays for softness or uncertainty-as-stance. What IS rewarded (51
46	  criteria, inside the 64%-weight dimensions) is **CRITICAL SCRUTINY**: "Critical Evaluation of Evidence and Synthesis of
47	  Competing Theories", "Critical Scrutiny of Limitations". **The judge does not want us to sound uncertain. It wants us to ADJUDICATE.**
48	- INSIGHT rubric, verbatim: "deep analysis and original insights rather than simply repeating known information";
49	  "clear logical reasoning and effectively explains causal relationships behind phenomena"; "identifies key issues ...
50	  provides insightful solutions"; "forward-looking thinking, can anticipate trends".
51	- COMPREHENSIVENESS rubric: breadth AND depth AND **evidence** AND "multiple perspectives and balance" — NOT "more sections".
52	- Structure: "Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow" is the largest readability criterion and explicitly names
53	  "distinct heading levels" / "effective headings and subheadings". Tables/charts: max ~2.8% of overall. Worth having, not worth optimising hard.
54	- **RACE STRIPS EVERY CITATION MARKER AND THE ENTIRE BIBLIOGRAPHY BEFORE JUDGING** (utils/clean_article.py, an LLM
55	  cleaner). VERIFIED: our 9,300 words -> 7,692 read; 345 [n] markers -> 0; 105-entry bibliography -> DELETED.
56	  **=> ALL bibliography/citation-quality work scores ZERO. Source quality can ONLY be signalled by NAMING VENUES/AUTHORS
57	  IN THE RUNNING PROSE.** (The 0.5102 reference ships ZERO inline citations.)
58	
59	## 4. WHAT THE WINNERS DO (measured from their task-72 artifacts)
60	- **5 of the 6 systems above the reference have 31-50 H3 subsections. Every system at/below the reference has ZERO.**
61	  (dalpha is the lone exception: 0 H3, but 38-word paragraphs.) **We have 0 H3 and 677-word paragraphs.**
62	- Winners' median paragraph: **59-75 words**. Reference: 131. OpenAI: 170. **Us: 677 — a different regime entirely.**
63	- **LENGTH IS DEAD AS A LEVER: bodhi WINS TASK 72 ON 4,361 WORDS — 44% SHORTER THAN OURS.** dalpha scores 0.5252 on 4,039.
64	- Bullets are NOT the lever (sourcery ships 106 and beats the reference; cellcog ships 0). Tables are NOT the lever (0-5).
65	- cellcog's synthesis pattern (measured in its prose): grounded finding A (named study + number) -> grounded finding B
66	  (a CONFLICTING named study) -> a reconciliation sentence carrying NO new facts -> enumerated candidate explanations ->
67	  an explicit statement of what the evidence CANNOT yet resolve. Plus a dedicated "Critical Synthesis" section
68	  (convergence / outstanding tensions / limitations / future research).
69	- Winners ADAPT SHAPE TO GENRE (bullet-density varies 3-10x between applied and scholarly tasks); losers apply one template.
70	
71	## 5. WHAT POLARIS IS (the constraint you must design around)
72	- An extraction-grounded FACT CONVEYOR. `verify_sentence_provenance` (provenance_generator.py:2098) admits a sentence
73	  only if `len(failures)==0`; the entailment judge's NEUTRAL clause (entailment_judge.py:588) kills any sentence that
74	  "introduces a fact, entity, **MECHANISM** ... NOT present in the SPAN".
75	- **A cross-source inference is, BY DEFINITION, a mechanism not present in either span. Our own gate deletes exactly the
76	  sentence type that earns INSIGHT (0.32 — the heaviest weight, and our worst dimension).**
77	- Boundary, precisely: a CONJUNCTION of two sources ("X found 2.1% [a] while Y found 0.4% [b]") PASSES. Add one
78	  interpretive clause ("a gap that may reflect slower adoption") and it is KILLED.
79	- **FAITHFULNESS IS THE MOAT AND IS NOT FOR SALE.** No fabricated fact, number, quote or attribution may ever ship. Any
80	  design that can leak one is unshippable, whatever it scores. We have a working deterministic contract
81	  (scripts/reflow_report.py): FACT (span-verified, byte-identical) / STRUCTURE (assembled from sidecar data) /
82	  INTERPRETATION (no digits, no new proper nouns, no citations, must sit beside >=2 facts from >=2 sources,
83	  contradiction-screened fail-closed). **NOTE: it was built around HEDGING — and the rubric does not reward hedging.
84	  It rewards ADJUDICATION. That contract needs rethinking, not just retuning.**
85	
86	## 6. MEASUREMENT (fixed tonight — use it, never score n=1 again)
87	- Judge noise: **SD = 0.0074** (byte-identical artifact scored 6x). Baseline is tighter (SD 0.0020).
88	- k=5 paired => SE(diff) = 0.0047 => **smallest resolvable effect = +0.0094 at 2 sigma.**
89	- Generation noise (re-composing the same config) is LARGER (~0.016) and applies ONLY to arms needing a new compose.
90	  A report->report transform of a BANKED artifact has ZERO generation noise — cheap AND sensitive. Prefer it.
91	- Our own history: baseline 0.4062 -> rank10 0.4382 = **+0.032, z=10** (real). The depth levers DID work, modestly.
92	  But every subsequent lever (padding removal, source firewall) scored FLAT. And ALL of it was aimed at proxies.
93	
94	## 7. YOUR TASK
95	Design the plan that takes POLARIS from **0.4382** to **the top of this board (0.54-0.56)**.
96	- Ground every lever in the rubric text or a measured artifact. NO PROXIES — we lost a night to them.
97	- State, for each lever: the criterion it moves, the expected points, how it FAILS, and the cheap test that proves the

99	- Respect the faithfulness moat absolutely. If a lever needs ungrounded prose, say so explicitly and justify the exact
100	  containment.
101	- Be honest about the ceiling. If your plan does not reach 0.54, SAY SO and name the residual.
102	- Where you are UNCERTAIN, SEARCH (papers, GitHub, the artifacts on disk at
103	  /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/). Do not guess.

TASK 72 PROMPT:
Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}

=== COMPREHENSIVENESS (weight 0.29) ===
- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.

=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.

=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.

=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
- [w=0.10] D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized :: Evaluates the clarity with which quantitative or qualitative data, empirical findings, or specific evidence from cited studies (e.g., statistics on automation rates, survey results on worker sentiment) are described, interpreted, or summarized within the review. If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness in aiding comprehension.
- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
- [w=0.10] A1: Audience Adaptation and Explanation of Terms :: Assesses if the language, level of detail, and style of argumentation are appropriate for an academic audience interested in AI's impact on the labor market. Evaluates whether potentially ambiguous or highly specialized terms are sufficiently contextualized or briefly explained to ensure understanding by readers who may not be experts in every specific sub-domain discussed (e.g., specific AI algorithms or niche economic theories).

content.text:
I have enough. Let me lock the verdict.

The decisive evidence:

**1. Half the rubric basis does not exist.** `grep -ic "limitation" race_task72_criteria.txt` → **0**. "Critical Scrutiny of Limitations" is not a task-72 criterion. It appears only in *our own brief* (`SOTA_BRIEF_V2.md:47`) as a description of the corpus-wide pool. Only "Value and Foresight..." grades us here, at effective weight **0.32 × 0.15 = 0.048**.

**2. The arithmetic kills the claim.** Grader confirmed: `overall = target_total/(target+reference)`, criterion weights within insight sum to 1.0. With S_ref≈9.0, S_ours≈7.02:

| raw gain on this one criterion | overall |
|---|---|
| +2.0 | +0.0033 |
| +3.0 (Good→Excellent) | +0.0050 |
| **+6.0** | **+0.0099** |

The claimed **+0.010 requires +6.0 raw points on a single criterion** — going from ~4 to a **perfect 10**, above the reference's ~9. And the realistic figure (+0.005) sits **below the brief's own smallest resolvable effect of +0.0094 (k=5, 2σ)**. The lever cannot be tested as an arm, and cannot deliver its top end.

**3. It skips the criterion's first-named conjunct.** The rubric reads "distill strategically valuable **implications** (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) **and** clearly delineate critical research gaps." The CHANGE ships methodological strength/limitation pairs + open problems — the *second* conjunct only. sourcery ships an entire `## Implications` section (policy / industrial strategy / inequality / monetary policy / institutional design); the reference ships `9.1 Consolidated Findings / 9.2 Ongoing Debates / 9.3 Future Research Directions`. Missing a named conjunct caps you at a better 7 — and the brief is explicit that there is no credit for a better 7.

**4. Wrong instrument.** bodhi's `8.3 Major open problems` items each carry **two citation markers**; the reference's is titled "Future Research Directions **Identified in the Literature**." The highest-scoring version of this section is **CLASS F/F+A** — each paper's *own* stated limitation, span-verified and attributed. dalpha's §11 ("exposure is not adoption"; "adoption is not impact") is the only class-I-shaped part, and it is ~6 sentences — comfortably inside the **existing 0.08 budget**. So L6 is riding a **0.08 → 0.35 class-I budget expansion (4.4×)** that its own exemplars show it does not need.

**5. Double-booked.** The `[91]` deletion is explicitly credited to L1/L5.

REFUTED as claimed (the goal survives; the lever's magnitude, rubric basis, scope, and instrument do not).

NOT a classic proxy — credit where due. "Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas" is verbatim in race_task72_criteria.txt, and the content spec is empirically well-calibrated: dalpha's "11. Methodological Limitations and Evidence Gaps" is almost exactly the proposed artifact ("First, exposure is not adoption... Second, adoption is not impact... Third, task-level productivity is not the same as labor-market welfare") — digit-free, entity-free, cited to >=2 sources — and dalpha scores 0.5252 on 4,039 words. We ship literally zero of this (our old Limitations was pipeline-telemetry-driven, per workspace/POLARIS/architecture.md:275). The gap is real.

But the LEVER as stated is refuted on five independent counts:

(1) HALF THE RUBRIC BASIS IS A PHANTOM. `grep -ic "limitation" /home/polaris/polaris_project/race_task72_criteria.txt` returns 0; "scrutiny" returns 0. "Critical Scrutiny of Limitations" is NOT a task-72 criterion — it exists only as a sentence in our own SOTA_BRIEF_V2.md:47 describing the corpus-wide criteria pool. Citing it roughly doubles the lever's apparent rubric footprint. Only "Value and Foresight" grades us, at effective weight 0.32 x 0.15 = 0.048.

(2) THE ARITHMETIC KILLS THE CLAIMED RANGE. Verified against the grader (utils/score_calculator.py: total = sum_dim w_dim * weighted-avg of 0-10 criteria; race.py:160 overall = target/(target+reference)). Insight criterion weights sum to 1.00, so eff. weight = 0.048. With S_ref≈9.0 and S_ours≈7.02 (reproduces 0.4382): +1 raw pt = +0.0017 overall; +3 = +0.0050; +6 = +0.0099. So the claimed +0.010 requires SIX raw points on one criterion — from ~4 to a PERFECT 10, i.e. above the reference's ~9. Arithmetically dead. And the realistic figure (Good 5 -> Excellent 8, +3) = +0.005, which is BELOW the brief's own smallest resolvable effect of +0.0094 at k=5, 2-sigma. The lever therefore cannot deliver its top end and cannot be validated at its realistic end. This is the exact signature of the levers that already scored FLAT (padding removal, source firewall).

(3) IT SKIPS THE CRITERION'S FIRST-NAMED CONJUNCT. The rubric text is a conjunction: "distill strategically valuable IMPLICATIONS (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) AND clearly delineate critical research gaps." The CHANGE ships (a) methodological strength/limitation pairs and (b) five open problems — both are the SECOND conjunct. Nothing addresses implications. sourcery ships a whole "## Implications" section (For Labor Market Policy / Industrial Strategy / Inequality / Monetary Policy / Institutional Design); the reference ships "9. Synthesis: Major Conclusions, Debates, and Future Research Agendas" (9.1 Consolidated Findings / 9.2 Ongoing Debates / 9.3 Future Research Directions). Missing a named conjunct caps this criterion at a better 7 — and the brief is explicit there is no credit for a better 7.

(4) WRONG INSTRUMENT, AND IT SMUGGLES A BUDGET EXPANSION IT DOESN'T NEED. Measured from the artifacts: bodhi's "8.3 Major open problems for research" items each carry TWO citation markers; the reference titles its subsection "9.3 Future Research Directions IDENTIFIED IN THE LITERATURE" — i.e. gaps the cited papers state about themselves. For POLARIS that is a CLASS F / F+A move (span-verified, byte-identical, attributable), needing NO class-I machinery, no vacuity check, no contradiction screen. dalpha's inferential-gap pairs are the only class-I-shaped content and they run ~6 sentences — comfortably inside the EXISTING I_BUDGET_FRAC=0.08. So if L6 is the argument for I_BUDGET_FRAC 0.08 -> 0.35 (4.4x, the single most fabrication-exposed change in the contract), that argument fails on L6's own exemplars.

(5) THE QUALITY GATE IS ITSELF A PROXY (the relapse, one level down). The lever's only defense against its own admitted failure mode ("generic appraisal scores zero") is the vacuity check = content-stem overlap with >=2 premises. Lexical stem overlap certifies GROUNDEDNESS, not VALUE. The criterion pays for "strategically valuable" and "promising." A stem-overlap check passes "Further research should examine how exposure indices relate to realized employment" exactly as happily as a genuinely valuable gap. The lever thus reproduces the winners' section INVENTORY (bodhi's "open problems", WhaleCloud's "6.4 Future Research Agendas") while its gate cannot enforce the substance those sections carry. That is surface imitation with a lexical proxy standing in for insight.

(6) DOUBLE-BOOKED. Deleting the "[91] AI-generated literature reviews are rejected" sentence is explicitly assigned to L1/L5; counting it inside L6's +0.005-0.010 inflates L6.

Also note the correlational evidence is the sourcery-bullets trap: 6/6 systems at/above the reference have a limitations/gaps/agenda apparatus and 0/3 below it do — but every one of those 6 is also denser, more structured, and better on all four dimensions. n=10, fully collinear. It is not evidence of a causal lever; it is evidence of a hygiene floor.

Bottom line: honest value is +0.002 to +0.005 (a hygiene fix, not a lever), it is unmeasurable standalone, and as written it under-serves its own criterion while paying for a class-I budget expansion it does not require.

2. NEVER MEASURE IT ALONE. Its ceiling (+0.005) is below the k=5 resolution floor (+0.0094). Fold L6 into a bundle with the other insight levers and score the bundle; a standalone FLAT result on L6 would be uninformative and would burn a 65-minute compose.

3. FLIP THE SENTENCE CLASS from I to F/F+A. The reference's top-scoring subsection is "Future Research Directions IDENTIFIED IN THE LITERATURE" and bodhi's open problems each cite two sources. Harvest each source's OWN stated limitation / future-work sentence from its Discussion section and ship them span-verified and attributed ("Acemoglu and Restrepo note that ... remains unidentified"). 5. DO NOT LET L6 CARRY THE I_BUDGET_FRAC 0.08 -> 0.35 EXPANSION. dalpha's exposure-is-not-adoption pairs are ~6 sentences and fit inside the existing 0.08. If the 4.4x budget increase is justified, it must be justified by the Critical Synthesis lever (L4) on its own evidence, not smuggled in here.

6. CHEAP PRE-COMPOSE TEST (proves the mechanism before paying): take the BANKED rank10 artifact, hand-splice in dalpha's §11-shaped block (built from our own corpus) plus a 5-item Implications block, and re-score k=5 as a report->report transform (zero generation noise, per brief §6).
